It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE created a new G frame at rows 32-35, cols 20-23 (between white at col 24 and target at cols 16-19). White block is adjacent to this G frame. Original target at cols 16-19 still uncollected.
- Goal model: Collect all three blue-square targets by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
- Action model: Movement moves player 4 cells. SPACE collects adjacent targets.
- Plan: Press SPACE to collect the G frame at cols 20-23, then move LEFT to activate the target at cols 16-19.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the current state after pressing SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current nodes:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Player position
green = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
white = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"\nGreen: {green[0]['boundary'] if green else 'NOT FOUND'}")
print(f"White: {white[0]['boundary'] if white else 'NOT FOUND'}")

# Target frames
c_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"\nTarget frames (c, 12px): {len(c_nodes)}")
for cn in c_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={cn['boundary']}")

# Check for G frames
G_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"G frames: {len(G_nodes)}")
for gn in G_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={gn['boundary']}")

# Blue centers
b_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"Blue centers (4px): {len(b_nodes)}")
for bn in b_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={bn['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After SPACE:
- Green: rows 32-35, cols 25-27 (unchanged)
- White: rows 32-35, cols 20-24 (expanded from col 24 to cols 20-24, now 4x5 = 16 pixels)
- G frame at cols 20-23 is GONE (collected!)
- Target at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 still exists (c frame)
- Blue center at rows 33-34, cols 21-22 is INSIDE the white block (collected)
- Blue center at rows 33-34, cols 17-18 is still in the target frame
So the G frame at cols 20-23 was collected. The white block now occupies cols 20-24. The target at cols 16-19 is still uncollected.
Now I need to activate the target at cols 16-19. The white block is at cols 20-24. The target is at cols 16-19. The white block's leftmost col is 20, and the target's rightmost col is 19. They're adjacent (col 19 is next to col 20).
Wait, the white block is at cols 20-24, and the target is at cols 16-19. The white block's leftmost col is 20, and the target's rightmost col is 19. So they're adjacent! The white block is to the RIGHT of the target.
So the white block is already adjacent to the target. I just need to press SPACE to collect it!
Wait, but in previous cases, the target changed to G BEFORE pressing SPACE. In this case, the target is still 'c'. Let me think...
Actually, looking at the pattern more carefully:
- In the first collection, the target changed to G when the player moved into it (green overlapping the target).
- In the second collection, the target changed to G when the white block was adjacent to it.
- In the third and fourth collections, the target changed to G when the white block was adjacent to it.
In all cases, the target changed to G when the white block was adjacent to it. But in this case, the white block is adjacent to the target, and the target is still 'c'.
Hmm, maybe the target only changes to G when the white block is adjacent AND the green block is in the right position. Or maybe the target only changes to G when the player moves in a specific way.
Wait, let me re-examine. In the fourth collection:
- After moving LEFT, the green block was at cols 41-43 and the white block was at col 40. The target was at cols 36-39. The white block at col 40 was adjacent to the target at col 39. The target changed to G.
In the current case:
- The white block is at cols 20-24. The target is at cols 16-19. The white block at col 20 is adjacent to the target at col 19. But the target is still 'c'.
The difference is that in the fourth collection, the white block was a single column (col 40), while in the current case, the white block is 5 columns wide (cols 20-24). Maybe the target only activates when the white block is a single column?
Or maybe the target only activates when the player moves in a way that causes the white block to be adjacent to the target. In the current case, the white block became adjacent to the target because of the SPACE action (which expanded the white block), not because of a movement action.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see if it collects the target.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: G frame at cols 20-23 collected! White block expanded to cols 20-24. Target at cols 16-19 still uncollected but white block is now adjacent to it (col 20 next to col 19).
Plan: Press SPACE to try collecting the target at cols 16-19.
